definition: The sequence of records created when a standard catalog item is ordered: Request (sc_request) -> Requested Item (sc_req_item) -> Catalog Task (sc_task).
To move an update set to a new instance, the exact sequence of actions is: Retrieve -> Preview -> Commit.
To pass an ACL and gain access, a user must meet all defined criteria in the rule: Roles, Conditions, and Scripts.
A personal VTB where lanes are created manually and task cards can be added freely. Moving cards between lanes does not update any underlying record data.

Changing the List Layout (via right-click > Configure) permanently changes the default view for all users. Changing the List Personalization (via the Gear Icon) overrides the default and only changes the view for the individual logged-in user.
A conversational bot platform that provides user assistance, answers FAQs, and resolves common issues through a messaging interface using Natural Language Understanding (NLU).
A specific type of business rule that executes before a form is loaded to the user. It is primarily used to pass server-side database data to client-side scripts using the g_scratchpad object.
Advanced styling records that use CSS variables or JSON to apply specific, granular branding. They can be selected by users as preferences (e.g., Dark Mode) or applied to specific workspaces.
A tool used to filter list data to extract specific records. It always consists of three components: Field, Operator, and Value.
ServiceNow's architecture model where every customer receives their own dedicated database and application logic. This ensures strict data isolation and allows for independent upgrade schedules.
A modular, grouped unit of variables that can be shared and reused across multiple different catalog items.
In progress -> Complete -> Ignore. (Note: Once an update set is marked 'Complete', it should never be reopened. Create a new one for fixes).
An elevated privilege role that must be manually granted per session. It is explicitly required to create or modify Access Control Lists (ACLs) and High Security Settings.
A VTB built from a list and driven by a specific field (like State). Moving a card from one lane to another automatically updates that field's value on the underlying record.

A feature that allows a field in an extended child table (e.g., Priority on Incident) to have a different behavior, mandatory status, or default value than the exact same field on its parent table (e.g., Task) without affecting other child tables.
Labels used to categorize, flag, and quickly locate records. Their visibility can be set to "Me" (personal), specific Groups/Users, or "Everyone" (global).
The modern ServiceNow user interface that consolidates access to the Application Navigator (All, Favorites, History), Workspaces, and Admin functions into a single top header menu.
The system table (often called the System Dictionary) that contains the definition for every table and column in the ServiceNow instance.
A centralized repository used for the creation, categorization, viewing, and governance of information and articles.
JavaScript. (ServiceNow uses it for both browser-based scripts and server-based scripts).
The security framework where ServiceNow is responsible for the platform's cloud infrastructure security, while the customer is responsible for data, user access, and endpoint security.
Reporting shows data exactly as it is at the current moment. Performance Analytics takes snapshots of data over time to track historical trends and forecast future performance.
The system table that contains the specific definition for every single column/field in the instance, dictating its underlying data type, character limit, and system behavior.
A temporary staging area that acts as an intermediate holding location for imported records before they are transformed into a target ServiceNow table.
System notifications can be configured to trigger in two primary ways: when a record is Inserted/Updated, or when an Event is fired.
A configuration setting that determines exactly where a Client Script or UI Policy is allowed to execute: Desktop (Standard UI), Mobile / Service Portal, or All.
The menu accessed by right-clicking a list's column header. It provides options to configure the list layout, export data, and create quick reports (bar/pie charts).

Persona-driven, single-pane-of-glass interfaces designed to help specific users (like Service Desk Agents) manage multiple tasks, view analytics, and interact with records efficiently without constantly switching pages.
A table that is extended by other tables, but does not extend any other table itself (e.g., the Task table).
Draft, Review, Publish, and Retire.
Client-side scripts run in the user's web browser (e.g., Client Scripts, UI Policies). Server-side scripts run directly on the ServiceNow database/servers (e.g., Business Rules, Script Includes).
A set of field maps that determines the relationships between fields in an Import Set table and fields in an existing target ServiceNow table.
The three main sections used to configure an email notification: When to send, Who will receive, and What it will contain.
A security mechanism that creates a namespace to protect an application's files and data from conflicting with, or being accidentally modified by, other applications on the platform.
The table that stores personalized UI changes made by a user (like personalizing a list by clicking the gear icon). This overrides global defaults for that specific user.
A URL command appended directly to the instance web address used to view critical system details, including the instance version, patch level, and the specific application node you are connected to.
Core tables exist out-of-the-box in the base system (e.g., Incident, User). Custom tables are created by admins or developers and always begin with the u_ prefix.
A record that defines conditions (based on roles, departments, groups, etc.) to evaluate who has access to read or contribute to Knowledge Bases and Service Catalog items.
A core server-side JavaScript API used to interact with the ServiceNow database (to query, insert, update, and delete records) without needing to write raw SQL.
A unified workspace interface that consolidates process automation tools, including Flow Designer, Integration Hub, Action Designer, and Playbooks.
A dynamic, no-code rule applied to a form to manage the behavior of fields. It handles three specific field states: Mandatory, Read-Only, and Visible.
A field used in a Transform Map to match imported data to existing records. If a match is found, the existing record is updated instead of creating a duplicate.
The Event Registry is where definitions of events reside. The Event Log is where actual triggered events are recorded by the system before the notification engine processes them.
The baseline application scope for legacy applications and core ServiceNow system processes. Custom tables created here traditionally use the u_ prefix.
Used to organize related fields together into separate, logical tabs or segmented areas on a single form.

Within the navigator, an Application is a grouping of related features (e.g., "Incident"), while a Module is a specific clickable link within that application that directs to a list, form, or page (e.g., "Create New" or "All").
A standard, prescriptive framework and data model for the CMDB that connects IT services to the underlying technical infrastructure.
The specific event or condition that initiates a flow in Flow Designer. Triggers can be Record-based (e.g., created/updated), Schedule-based, or Application-based.
The component of a UI Policy that defines the exact behavior applied to a target field (e.g., setting the Priority field to Read-Only) when the UI Policy conditions evaluate to true.
A server-side rule that enforces data consistency by making fields mandatory or read-only. It applies to all data entering the platform, whether via the UI, import sets, or web services.
A container that groups a set of customization changes so they can be moved entirely from one ServiceNow instance to another (e.g., from Development to Test, then to Production).
A form layout element used to divide a form section into two columns. Exactly one split is needed to create a two-column layout.
Searching in the "All" menu filter returns three specific things: names of Applications, names of Modules, and names of Favorites that match the query.
Foundation, Design, Build, Manage Technical, and Consume.
A tool accessed via the form header (More Options ...) that allows users to populate multiple predefined fields automatically, saving time and ensuring consistency.

A role that allows an administrator or designated user to log in as another user to test access and troubleshoot issues without needing their password. Crucial exam fact: You cannot impersonate users who hold the security_admin role.
cmdb_ci is the base table that stores the actual Configuration Items (assets/services). cmdb_rel_ci is the table that stores the directional relationships between those items.
A visual variable used in Flow Designer to dynamically drag-and-drop data from a trigger or a previous action into a subsequent action.

Client scripts only run in the browser and trigger on four specific events: onLoad (when a form loads), onChange (when a specific field is modified), onSubmit (when the save/submit button is clicked), and onCellEdit (when a list field is edited).
A robust ordering system for services and products offered by various departments, organized into categories and subcategories.

The baseline update set that automatically captures customizations if you haven't selected a specific one. Crucial exam fact: Best practice dictates the Default update set should never be moved between instances.

Tabs at the bottom of a form that display records in other tables that have a relationship to the current record (e.g., Incident Tasks on an Incident). Crucial exam fact: They only appear after a new record has been saved to the database.

Software components that provide additional features and functionalities within a ServiceNow instance. Crucial exam fact: Once a plugin is activated, it cannot be completely deleted or deactivated (though its modules can be hidden).
A security rule defined at the table or field level that restricts access to data and operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete).
A single, reusable operation executed within a flow, such as creating a record, sending an email, or requesting an approval.
A server-side script that executes when a database action occurs—specifically when records are queried, inserted, updated, or deleted.
A specific type of catalog item that translates user input into a task-based record (like an Incident or Change Request) rather than generating a requested item.

term: Data Not Captured in Update Sets
definition: Update sets capture structural changes (configurations), but they do not capture operational data (like Incidents, Users, Groups) or Configuration Items (CIs).
A tool that evaluates user responses to initial questions to automatically bundle and submit a single request containing multiple related catalog items (e.g., Employee Onboarding).
The specific system table where all individual customization payloads (Customer Updates) are stored while they belong to an update set.

A core base table that provides standard fields (like Assignment Group, Priority, State) and platform features (like SLAs and Approvals) to all its extended child tables (Incident, Problem, Change, etc.).
The core system table that stores global configuration settings, system-wide preferences, and behavioral toggles (like default date formats or attachment size limits).
The system evaluates Table-level ACLs first (from most specific to most general), followed by Field-level ACLs (from most specific to most general).
A scoped application containing predefined Flow Designer actions designed to integrate with and execute commands on a specific third-party system (e.g., Microsoft AD Spoke, Jira Spoke).
Business rules can be configured to run at four different times relative to the database action: Before, After, Async, or Display.
A developer tool used to visually drag and drop Now Experience web components onto a page to build custom workspaces and modern interfaces.
Brings everything under one unified top navigation bar (All, Favorites, History, Workspaces), a contextual app pill, global search, and native support for Dark Theme.
The Now Experience is the underlying developer framework used to build modern components. The Next Experience is the overarching user interface and navigation shell that users actually see.
A developer-focused, web-component-based architecture used as the engine to build modern Workspaces and custom portals (replacing older Jelly scripting/Angular).

Introduced in the San Diego release, it is the unified navigation shell that allows users to seamlessly transition between classic "Core UI" forms and modern Workspaces without opening new browser tabs.
A simplified interface used to apply global branding (like banner images, browser tab titles, and core system colors) across the instance without requiring CSS knowledge.
